questions.text: I hold the view that a steady emotional baseline is simply the way I experience the world.
I believe that the quietness of my emotional life is a feature of my constitution, not something to be overcome.
I find that the moral pull of suffering is the experience that has most profoundly shaped my sense of obligation to the people and communities I belong to.
I find that I am most motivated when I am working toward something that benefits me directly.
I deal with warranty and receipt issues when they arise rather than maintaining records.
I believe that awareness of suffering creates a moral duty to act, not merely to acknowledge.
I am easily brought to goosebumps by music, words, or images that strike the right chord.
I think that the capacity to remain logically oriented during emotionally difficult moments is what makes some people reliable in a crisis.
I find that I naturally track the undercurrents of group dynamics.
I tend to go all-in emotionally, whether the feeling is positive or negative.
I rarely sense when a social environment has an implicit dress code or behavioral code that I am violating.
I prefer making decisions quickly so I can start learning from the outcomes.
I can sense when someone is holding a grudge even though they are acting normally.
I trust that the group will find its way forward without one person driving.
I find it natural to defer my own interests when group needs arise.
I believe clarity of vision matters more than everyone's agreement.
I notice that my emotional highs are exhilarating but my lows are equally deep.
I find it satisfying to maintain cohesion as the group moves forward.
I feel that groups anchor themselves when every member has genuine influence.
I find that I do not automatically sense when someone is emotionally exhausted even if they are carrying on normally.
I'm comfortable with ambiguity in group projects and trust things will sort themselves out.
I hold the view that the simplest acts of service—holding a door, carrying a bag—are among the most meaningful.
I feel frustrated when leadership is unclear or absent.
I find it difficult to tell when a person's cheerfulness is forced.
I think that my ability to remain composed when others are in distress is a quality that has made me a more reliable person.
I feel that my energy is best spent making sure nobody around me is struggling alone.
I treat my calendar as a binding contract with myself.
I routinely treat myself to experiences and products that bring me personal joy.
I feel that healthy self-focus gives me the strength to show up fully in everything I do.
I believe that structured, rational engagement with emotionally difficult situations is the approach most likely to minimize harm and maximize benefit.
I maintain a reading list and work through it systematically.
I find it easy to overrule suggestions that do not align with my direction.
I notice when a person touches their face or neck during a conversation as a self-soothing gesture.
My baseline state is one of such stability that disruptions feel like small departures rather than upheavals.
I feel that the depth of my caring for others is something I would never want to diminish.
I am careful to double-check my work for errors before submitting it.
